U1.evidence: In .goalloop-support/triage9/U1: facts.json = valid v2 facts, one frame ["T","abb"], one sample value 1; config.json = {"T": {"Hit": "regex:(?x)# (\n(?P<a>a)(?P<b>b)\\2"}}. `uv run clankerprof targets --facts facts.json --config config.json --format json` -> exit 0, parents.T.categories[0].name = "Hit" (time_ns 1). `target/debug/clankerprof-rs targets --facts facts.json --config config.json --format json` -> exit 0, categories[0].name = "Other". Mechanism verified in crates/clankerprof-core/src/targets.rs:487-533 (rewrite_numeric_backrefs_to_named): the scanner skips `(?#...)` comment groups (skip_regex_comment, per the R8-08 fix) but has no notion of `(?x)` extended mode, so the `(` inside the `# (` line comment is counted as group 1, shifting a->2, b->3; `\2` is then rewritten to `\k<a>`, silently changing the pattern to require "aba" instead of "abb". Spec docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md ~530-545 promises "the rewrite understands CPython `(?#…)` comment groups: parentheses inside comments never count toward group numbering" and pins only engine MATCHING edges ($ before trailing newline, (?i) special folds, \w/\s/\b class edges, conditional groups) as outside the parity guarantee. An extended-mode `#` comment is not among the documented exceptions, and this is a compiler-induced semantic change in the rewrite itself, which the spec explicitly claims "compiles identically". Silent wrong attribution, exit 0 in both — parity break on a valid Python-dialect pattern.
U1.subclaims: 1) Python treats `# (` under (?x) as comment text -> Hit: reproduced. 2) Rust counts the comment parenthesis as a capture group and rewrites \2 to group a -> Other: reproduced, mechanism confirmed at targets.rs rewrite scanner (no x-mode handling). 3) Not covered by documented engine exceptions: confirmed — spec pins matching-semantics edges only and separately asserts the rewrite is numbering-faithful. 4) Exit 0 both sides (silent): confirmed.
U1.fix_sketch: Rust-only (Python is the reference and is correct). In rewrite_numeric_backrefs_to_named (crates/clankerprof-core/src/targets.rs): track verbose mode — when a global inline-flag group `(?...x...)` (flag letters then `)`, no `:`; Python requires global flags at pattern start) is seen, set x-mode; while x-mode is on and outside a character class, a `#` starts a comment consumed through the next `\n` (or end of pattern) in BOTH the group-counting pass and the rewrite pass (never rewrite digits inside comments). For scoped `(?x:...)`/`(?-x:...)` groups either implement a flag stack or conservatively return None so the original fancy-regex error fails closed with exit 2 (spec-sanctioned residual). fancy-regex itself honors (?x) `#` comments, so after a numbering-correct rewrite (\2 -> \k<b>) matching agrees with Python. Extend the spec sentence at docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md ~538 to say the rewrite also understands `(?x)` extended-mode `#` line comments, and add a parity test pinning the repro pattern to Hit/Hit byte-identically.

U2.evidence:
In .goalloop-support/triage9/U2. Fixture 1: facts1.json samples [Leaf,CallerA,Boundary]=+10 and [Leaf,CallerB,Boundary]=-10; scope1.yml = 'cost_kind:
  Work: "name:Leaf"
scope:
  - function: Boundary'. Both `uv run clankerprof scopes --facts facts1.json --config scope1.yml` and `target/debug/clankerprof-rs scopes --facts facts1.json --config scope1.yml` exit 0 with boundaries[0].buckets = [] and total_time_ns 0 — the Work category vanishes although its caller_leaf_pairs (CallerA->Leaf=+10, CallerB->Leaf=-10) are nonzero (the leaf_functions map cancels because both samples share leaf name Leaf, so the R8-01 subtree check passes it for omission). Fixture 2: facts2.json LeafA=+10/LeafB=-10 under Owner (path /own/o.rb), scope2.yml with owner 'D: "path:/own/**"'. Both CLIs exit 0 emitting the Work category (time_ns 0, signed leaf_functions, caller_leaf_pairs Owner->LeafA=10/Owner->LeafB=-10) but domains: [] — the domain's file stats key functions by the Owner frame (net 0) while the rendered per-file caller_leaf_pairs are nonzero. Mechanism: clankerprof/render.py:224-230 (_category_renderable checks stats.cpu_time and stats.functions only, but _render_boundary_category renders stats.caller_leaf_pairs at render.py:292-309) and render.py:206-219 (domain filter checks cpu_time/cost_kinds/files.functions, but _render_domain renders per-file caller_leaf_pairs at render.py:399+); identical in crates/clankerprof-core/src/scopes.rs:743-760 (domains filter) and scopes.rs:783-792 (category_renderable). Spec docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md ~596-606: "A row may be omitted only when its aggregate AND its entire rendered subtree are zero ... The rule applies at every level." Caller pairs are part of the rendered subtree, so both omissions violate the contract. Identical in both languages but harmful (signed evidence silently erased), which counts per the triage rules.
U2.subclaims: 1) Zero-sum category with nonzero caller pairs omitted (buckets []): reproduced in both languages, exit 0. 2) Zero-sum domain with nonzero per-file caller pairs omitted (domains []) while the sibling category renders its signed leaves: reproduced in both. 3) Filters ignore caller-pair maps the renderers expose: confirmed by reading _category_renderable/_render_boundary_category and the domain filter/_render_domain plus the Rust mirrors. 4) Violates the every-level rendered-subtree rule at SPEC ~599: confirmed.
U2.fix_sketch: Both languages, byte-identical. Python render.py: extend _category_renderable to `stats.cpu_time != 0 or any(m.cpu_time != 0 for m in stats.functions.values()) or any(m.cpu_time != 0 for m in stats.caller_leaf_pairs.values())`; extend the domain filter in _render_boundary to also check `any(m.cpu_time != 0 for fs in stats.files.values() for m in fs.caller_leaf_pairs.values())` (and per-file cost_kinds for completeness so every rendered leaf field participates: `any(m.cpu_time != 0 for fs in stats.files.values() for m in fs.cost_kinds.values())`). Rust scopes.rs: mirror the same predicates in category_renderable and the domains filter. Consider hoisting a shared 'nonzero rendered subtree' helper per struct to keep the two call sites honest. Add parity tests pinning both fixtures (bucket renders its zero-sum category with signed caller pairs; domain D renders), and extend the SPEC ~599 example list to name caller-to-leaf pairs as subtree rows.

U3.evidence: In .goalloop-support/triage9/U3. Case A: pack.yml = {schema_version v1, core_native_default_category: 'Native Core', native_path_patterns: ['regex:/native/']}; core.csv = 'Object'; config.json = {"T": {}}; facts.json leaf Object#new at /native/ext.so under T. `uv run clankerprof targets --facts facts.json --config config.json --runtime-rules pack.yml --core-classes core.csv --format json` -> exit 0, category ('Other', 10); Rust identical. Direct probe (load_runtime_rules_file('pack.yml', core_classes=['Object'])): `is_native_path: True` / `category: None` — exactly as claimed. Case B: pack2.yml with native_path_markers ['/native/'] and native_path_exclude_markers ['/native/app/'], frame at /native/app/object.rb -> both CLIs emit ('Native Core', 10) while is_native_path returns False. Mechanism: clankerprof/categorize.py:189-195 and crates/clankerprof-core/src/categorize.rs:129-136 compute explicit_native_path from pseudo-paths plus positive native_path_markers only, never calling is_native_path (clankerprof/patterns.py:168-182), which honors native_path_exclude_markers, native_path_exclude_patterns, and native_path_patterns. Contract: SPEC:268 assigns 'native/core/stdlib detection' to the rule pack; SPEC:330-333 keys core-class resolution on being 'on native paths' vs 'off native paths' with no marker-only carve-out; SPEC ~304-307 promises pack keys are never silently disabled. The shipped ruby.yml itself declares native_path_patterns (regex:/ruby/[0-9][^/]*) and native_path_exclude_markers (/gems/), both silently inert for core-class categorization. is_native_path is used everywhere else (scopes.py:280, slices.py:233, semantic ownership categorize.py:169), so the same path is 'native' to native: selectors/collapse but 'non-native' to the core table. git log -L shows the branch was moved verbatim in the analysis split (1fdfeab) — legacy code, not a documented deliberate asymmetry; no doc mentions the explicit-marker distinction. Identical in both languages but a genuine correctness defect against the pack contract.
U3.subclaims: 1) native_path_patterns ignored by core-class handling (Other instead of Native Core): reproduced in both languages plus direct probe is_native_path=True/category=None. 2) native-path exclusions bypassed (Native Core despite is_native_path False): reproduced in both. 3) Branch checks only pseudo paths + positive markers: confirmed at categorize.py:189-195 / categorize.rs:129-136. 4) Not a documented asymmetry: confirmed — no spec/doc text, and the shipped ruby pack relies on the ignored fields.
U3.fix_sketch: Both languages identically. categorize.py:189 / categorize.rs:130: replace the manual computation with `explicit_native_path = path == "<cfunc>" or (not path.startswith("<internal:") and is_native_path(path, rules))` (Rust: `path == "<cfunc>" || (!path.starts_with("<internal:") && is_native_path(path, rules))`), preserving the <internal:> routing to core_internal_categories and the <cfunc> fast path. Note one behavior edge to pin with a test: is_native_path('') is True while the old branch treated the empty path as non-native — either accept the new (native) classification in both languages together or add an explicit empty-path guard, identically. Add a SPEC sentence under the core-class section (~330) stating core-class native routing uses the pack's full native-path detection (markers, patterns, exclusions) with <internal:> taking precedence, plus parity tests for both repro packs (Native Core for pattern-matched paths; core_semantic/None for excluded paths).

U4.evidence: In .goalloop-support/triage9/U4: facts.json = /special/a.rb value 10 and /other/b.rb value 20; slices.yml = one non-default slice {name: "(all)", paths: ["/special/**"], metadata: {intended: special-only}}. `uv run clankerprof slices --facts facts.json --slices slices.yml` and `target/debug/clankerprof-rs slices --facts facts.json --slices slices.yml` both exit 0 with a single identical row: name "(all)", time_ns 30, pct 100.0, BOTH frames (/other/b.rb 20 + /special/a.rb 10), metadata {intended: special-only}, and — beyond the claim — "is_default": true although default was never declared. Mechanism: clankerprof/slices.py:187 hardcodes `self._default_slice = "(all)"`, so the implicit unmatched-sample fallback and the configured row share one string key and merge; reserved-name validation at slices.py:345-347 (mirrored in slices.rs) rejects only "(gc)" and "(uncollapsible)". Contract: SPEC:641 defines `(all)` as the destination for unmatched samples (a pseudo-output role); SPEC:678-687 reserves (gc)/(uncollapsible) precisely because a colliding user slice 'would be attributed and then stripped', i.e. pseudo-output collisions are treated as validation errors — but the third pseudo-name is unreserved; CLANKERPROF_V2_PLAN.md item 35 already flagged `(all)` as an under-specified pseudo-slice. Result is a silently wrong artifact: the row claims 100% under configured 'special-only' metadata while its path rule matches only 10 of 30 units, and it masquerades as the default slice. Identical in both languages, harmful, undocumented — confirmed.
U4.subclaims: 1) Configured non-default "(all)" merges with the implicit fallback, absorbing non-matching samples: reproduced (30/100% with both frames). 2) Configured metadata retained on the merged row: reproduced. 3) Only (gc)/(uncollapsible) reserved: confirmed at slices.py:345-347 and SPEC:679. 4) Extra harm not in the claim: is_default flips to true without a default declaration. 5) Both languages identical, exit 0: reproduced.
U4.fix_sketch: Reserve "(all)" alongside the existing pseudo-names in both languages: extend the reserved-name validation (clankerprof/slices.py ~345, crates/clankerprof-core/src/slices.rs equivalent) and the shared message to 'The names (all), (gc) and (uncollapsible) are reserved for analyzer pseudo-outputs.', keeping the message byte-identical across languages. Because the check lives in slice-definition validation, the R8-07 scope-config slice loading and the R7-01 unconditional --attribute reservation inherit it — extend the attribute-target reserved set with the same constant so `--attribute 'name:x=(all)'` also fails closed. Update SPEC:679 to name all three reserved pseudo-outputs (documenting that `(all)` is the implicit fallback row), touch docs/CLANKERPROF_PARITY.md, and add parity + compliance tests asserting the identical exit-2 envelope for a configured `(all)` slice in YAML/TOML and via --attribute.

U5.evidence: In .goalloop-support/triage9/U5: facts.json = valid v2 facts, one frame T, two samples each value 1 with sample_index 7 on BOTH (summary self-consistent: sample_count 2, total 2). `uv run clankerprof targets --facts facts.json --target T --format json` -> exit 0, total_time_ns 2, [('Other', samples 2)]; `target/debug/clankerprof-rs targets --facts facts.json --target T --format json` -> identical exit 0 report. Mechanism: clankerprof/facts.py:683-687 (_sample_index) only checks non-negative <= u64 max and facts.py:291-320 assigns it without comparing to the enumeration position; crates/clankerprof-core/src/facts.rs:325-355 does the same via sample_index_field. Contract: SPEC:30 (decoder preserves 'sample order and stable zero-based sample_index') and SPEC:137 ('Stable zero-based index from the original profile order') give the field a normative meaning that indexes {7,7} for positions {0,1} contradict; SPEC:106 declares samples an 'Ordered sample fact array'. The importer-validation list at SPEC ~157-167 does not yet require positional equality, but nothing documents the leniency as deliberate, and this is exactly the class the project confirmed in R7-02/R8-14 (accepting an artifact field that contradicts its own documented derivation, since exporters can only ever produce enumerate-derived indexes — clankerprof/model.py:213, facts.rs export). No projection consumes sample_index today, so severity is minor as claimed: harm is accepting malformed facts as valid and carrying meaningless indexes through the strict contract. Confirmed.
U5.subclaims: 1) Both strict v2 importers accept duplicate/non-positional sample_index: reproduced, exit 0 both. 2) Only a range check exists (facts.py:683, facts.rs sample_index_field): confirmed by reading both. 3) Normative meaning is stable zero-based profile order: confirmed at SPEC:30/137. 4) Severity minor (no downstream consumer, no parity divergence): confirmed — grep shows no projection reads sample_index.
U5.fix_sketch: Both languages, identical message. Python facts.py _sample_facts_from_v2: iterate `for position, item in enumerate(raw_samples)` and after parsing raise `ValueError(f"Sample fact sample_index {parsed} does not match its profile-order position {position}.")` when they differ (duplicates fall out automatically). Rust facts.rs: same check in the v2 sample loop using the running position, byte-identical message. Keep legacy v1 imports lenient (historical leniency is documented). Add a bullet to the SPEC importer-validation list (~157-167): importers reject a v2 sample_index that differs from the sample's zero-based position. Add a parity test asserting the identical exit-2 error envelope for the duplicate-index fixture and for a 1-based fixture.
